title: Example post — MapReduce: como Jeffrey Dean e Sanjay Ghemawat criaram o modelo que permitiu ao Google processar petabytes em maquinas comuns
body:
🇺🇸 In 2004, Jeffrey Dean and Sanjay Ghemawat published a paper describing how Google ran massive computations across thousands of commodity machines. The model had two steps: map a function over every record to produce intermediate key-value pairs, then reduce those pairs by key into a final result. Nothing in that description was new. Functional programmers had used map and fold for decades. What Dean and Ghemawat built was the infrastructure that hid distribution entirely, so an engineer writing a word count program did not need to think about network partitions, machine failures, or data shuffling. Hadoop brought the model to the rest of the industry in 2006. Spark later showed that keeping data in memory across stages could run jobs ten times faster, but kept the same conceptual model. What computational model have you adopted that hides complexity you are glad not to think about?

---

🇧🇷 Em 2004, Jeffrey Dean e Sanjay Ghemawat publicaram um artigo descrevendo como o Google rodava computacoes massivas em milhares de maquinas comuns. O modelo tinha dois passos: aplicar uma funcao em cada registro para produzir pares chave-valor intermediarios, depois reduzir esses pares por chave em um resultado final. O que Dean e Ghemawat construiram foi a infraestrutura que escondia a distribuicao por completo. O Hadoop trouxe o modelo para o resto da industria em 2006. Qual modelo computacional voce adotou que esconde uma complexidade que voce fica feliz em nao precisar pensar?
